pattern: On a directed acyclic graph you can propagate, in topological order, a per-color running maximum count along the best path ending at each node; a cycle (detectable because topological processing cannot consume every node) makes the answer undefined.
hints: If the graph has a cycle there is no longest path, so first decide whether the graph is acyclic at all.
Carry along each path a count of how many nodes of every one of the 26 colors it has seen, and keep, per node, the best such counts over all paths ending there.

Process nodes in topological order: a node's counts are the elementwise maximum of its predecessors' counts plus one for the node's own color; if you cannot order all nodes, a cycle exists and the answer is -1.

edge_inputs.description: two nodes of the same color in a chain
disconnected nodes with no edges each contribute one
branching where the most frequent color path is not the longest path
a cycle hidden inside a larger graph still forces -1
diamond where two paths reconverge and one accumulates more of one color

stress.description: random acyclic graph (edges only go to higher indices) so the answer is always defined
stress.note: DAG-by-construction keeps the longest-path answer well defined

stress.description: random graph that may contain cycles to exercise the -1 path
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    colors = ''.join(chr(ord('a') + rng.randint(0, 2)) for _ in range(n))
    edges = []
    for _ in range(rng.randint(0, n * 2)):
        u = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        v = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        edges.append([u, v])
    return [colors, edges]
stress.note: free-form edges produce a mix of cyclic and acyclic cases
